topic: Saving a Lean game solution in plain text for later review
author: Eduardo Cavazos
content:
Snir Broshi said:

Eduardo Cavazos said:

If y'all know of another or better way, suggestions welcome.

Click the editor button on the top right, then copy all the code

Hey @Snir Broshi :man_raising_hand:
Editor mode provides the code, but without the intermediate goal states. So it looks something like this:
apply Iff.intro
intro h1
intro s
intro h2
intro x
intro h3
apply h1

With the approach I demonstrate in the video, I get the intermediate states:
Active Goal
Objects:
U: Type
A: Set U
F: Set (Set U)
⋃₀ F ⊆ A ↔ ∀ s ∈ F, s ⊆ A
apply Iff.intro

Active GoalGoal 2
Objects:
U: Type
A: Set U
F: Set (Set U)
⋃₀ F ⊆ A → ∀ s ∈ F, s ⊆ A
intro h1

Active GoalGoal 2
Objects:
U: Type
A: Set U
F: Set (Set U)
Assumptions:
h1: ⋃₀ F ⊆ A
∀ s ∈ F, s ⊆ A
intro s

...

topic: Saving a Lean game solution in plain text for later review
author: Eduardo Cavazos
content:
Another benefit of this format...
It makes it straightforward to search previous solutions for a symbol to see how I handled it... Importantly, it lets me search both the goals and the code used to transform the goals.
Without this, I had to manually look through solutions from previous levels to see how I handled a setup with a particular symbol.

author: Michael
content:
For a,b,n∈Na, b, n \in \mathbb{N}a,b,n∈N, I have a∣na \mid na∣n and n∣b⋅nan \mid b \cdot \frac{n}{a}n∣b⋅an​. I'd like to show that a∣ba \mid ba∣b.
The proof in my head goes like this: since a∣na \mid na∣n, the rational number q=b⋅na∈Zq = b \cdot \frac{n}{a} \in \mathbb{Z}q=b⋅an​∈Z. Since n∣qn \mid qn∣q, the rational number qn∈Z\frac{q}{n} \in \mathbb{Z}nq​∈Z. Since qn=ba\frac{q}{n} = \frac{b}{a}nq​=ab​, we have ba∈Z\frac{b}{a} \in \mathbb{Z}ab​∈Z and therefore a∣ba \mid ba∣b.
But I'm wary of doing a proof that crosses types like this. Are there lemmas to do the same thing while staying in ℕ?
(And how would I most effectively discover them myself? I searched Loogle for ?a ∣ ?c → ?c ∣ ?b * (?c / ?a) → ?a ∣ ?b, which if I understand correctly is the exact thing I want, but I didn't get a matc

author: Snir Broshi
content: Isn't a = 2, b = 1, n = 4 a counterexample?

author: Snir Broshi
content: Since a ∣ n you have (n / a) * a = n (docs#Nat.div_mul_cancel), and if you set d := n / a you get a * d ∣ b * d which implies a ∣ b assuming d ≠ 0 (docs#Nat.dvd_of_mul_dvd_mul_left)

author: Michael
content: Thanks!

author: Snir Broshi
content: So I believe {a = 2, b = 1, n = 0} is a counterexample, but you just need to require n ≠ 0

author: Kevin Buzzard
content: You should formalise the statement you're trying to prove rather than asking it in natural language. That way it would be clearer about whether your division is pathological.

author: Kevin Buzzard
content: ie if you are concerned about "crossing types" how about you tell us the exact type of everything in your question? And the easiest way to do this is to formalise the statement.

author: Michael
content:
Kevin Buzzard said:

ie if you are concerned about "crossing types" how about you tell us the exact type of everything in your question?

Well, by comparison to my question:

For a,b,n∈Na, b, n \in \mathbb{N}a,b,n∈N


the rational number qqq


Are there lemmas to do the same thing while staying in ℕ?

I'm not really sure how I could have been more explicit or what quantity I mentioned that wasn't tagged with a type.

author: Kevin Buzzard
content:
I simply mean: don't say "I would like to prove this theorem which I'm describing in English", say "here is some code, i would like to fill in the sorry". Then what you're asking becomes unambiguous.
In general, writing lean code is the best way to ask a question on this forum.

author: symmetrybreaking

author: Eduardo Cavazos
content:
Set Theory Game
Family Combination World
Level 1 / 8 : Complement of a family union
Don't read the below if you don't want to see solutions yet. :-)
github solution
The solution as given in github uses the following step:
have h3a : tᶜ ∈ {A | Aᶜ ∈ F}


Their whole solution is here:


ext x
apply Iff.intro
intro h1
rewrite [mem_sInter]
intro t h2
rewrite [mem_setOf] at h2
rewrite [mem_compl_iff] at h1
rewrite [mem_sUnion] at h1
push_neg at h1
have h3 := h1 tᶜ h2
rewrite [mem_compl_iff] at h3
push_neg at h3
exact h3
intro h1
rewrite [mem_sInter] at h1
rewrite [mem_compl_iff]
rewrite [mem_sUnion]
push_neg
intro t h2
have h3 := h1 tᶜ
have h3a : tᶜ ∈ {A | Aᶜ ∈ F}
rewrite [mem_setOf]
rewrite [compl_compl]
exact h2
have h4 := h3 h3a
rewrite [mem_compl_iff] at h4
exact h4

alternative solution
The

topic: Regrouping summands in a direct sum decomposition
author: Marcus Zibrowius
content:
I believe I have formalized the following "pushforward of gradings along maps between indexing stets":

Given a module M over a ring R with a grading grM : ι₁ → Submodule R M, and given a map f : ι₁ → ι₂, we obtain an induced_grading : ι₂ → Submodule R M on the same module simply by "regrouping" the summands.  

But while the code compiles, there are still parts that look very ugly. And it took me a lot of trial and error to get there.  So I'm pretty sure I'm approaching some of this from the wrong angle.  I post the code below, and I would be grateful for some feedback.
More specific questions:

The key equivalence that "regroups" the summands is called sigmaFiberLinearEquiv below. In defining this, I need to move "indices" back and forth through Mathlib's sigmaFibreEquiv, and I just don'

author: Li Xuanji
content:
I assume my second example fails because of a missing annotation - how do I diagnose this?
import Mathlib

example (z : ℂ) : MeromorphicAt (fun i ↦ Complex.cosh i) z := by fun_prop
example (z : ℂ) : MeromorphicAt (fun i ↦ Complex.cosh (2 * i)) z := by fun_prop -- fails

author: ZhiKai Pong
content:
As someone who has no idea what MeromorphicAt means, here's my attempt:

put set_option trace.Meta.Tactic.fun_prop true before the statement
read trace saying 

  [] failed applying `MeromorphicAt` theorems for `Complex.cosh`
               trying again after decomposing function as: `(fun i0 => Complex.cosh i0) ∘ (fun i => 2 * i)`
  [] missing composition rule to prove `MeromorphicAt (fun i => Complex.cosh (2 * i)) z`


look for MeromorphicAt + compwith search tools (I just went to docs)
final proof:

example (z : ℂ) : MeromorphicAt (fun i ↦ Complex.cosh (2 * i)) z := by
  change MeromorphicAt ((fun i => Complex.cosh i) ∘ (fun i => 2 * i)) z
  apply MeromorphicAt.comp_analyticAt
  fun_prop

My guess is you can probably tag MeromorphicAt.comp_analyticAt with fun_prop, but I

author: Bhavik Mehta
content:
Adam Topaz said:

To make the question more explicit, what is an example of an argument about, say, Digraphs, which doesn’t have an analogue in algebra (where we are perfectly happy to use types)

Adding or removing an vertex to a graph is a completely normal thing to do to get a new graph, but adding or removing an element from a group gives you something basically useless.

author: Peter Nelson
content:
To elaborate a little further on my last comment: 
In that repo, Jun has formalized a good number of standard results for multigraphs that use minors: Menger's theorem, maintaining 3-connectivity with contraction, and graphic matroids. 
Getting to these has pretty thoroughly tested our implementation of minors for problems and it seems it is doing ok. Getting this into mathlib has been slow, but I don't think there are any conceptual barriers.

author: Jun Kwon
Snir Broshi
content: FYI #36210 "definitions of graph contraction and graph minor" (I haven't read it yet though)

author: Adam Topaz
content:
Bhavik Mehta said:

Adam Topaz said:

To make the question more explicit, what is an example of an argument about, say, Digraphs, which doesn’t have an analogue in algebra (where we are perfectly happy to use types)

Adding or removing an vertex to a graph is a completely normal thing to do to get a new graph, but adding or removing an element from a group gives you something basically useless.

Yes, I understand. I think a good analogy is localizing rings like I mentioned above. Anyway, it’s perfectly fine for graph theorists to formalize things in the way that’s convenient for them and for algebraists to do it differently.

author: Peter Nelson
content:
Bhavik Mehta said:

Adam Topaz said:

To make the question more explicit, what is an example of an argument about, say, Digraphs, which doesn’t have an analogue in algebra (where we are perfectly happy to use types)

Adding or removing an vertex to a graph is a completely normal thing to do to get a new graph, but adding or removing an element from a group gives you something basically useless.

Also, locally changing the value of a * b for a single pair of elements in a group is nonsense algebraically, but graph theorists do things like that all the time.

author: Adam Topaz
content: If you want to start using docs#Quiver as a model for directed multigraphs then you will need to coordinate with the category theory parts of mathlib

author: Adam Topaz
content: Does mathlib have anything about directed multigraphs? Is that something graph theorists think about?

author: Peter Nelson
content: Not yet, but it is being discussed as part of an effort to unify walks/paths for different graph-like objects.

author: Peter Nelson
content:
I wanted to make a couple more comments about this. 
First, I want to acknowledge that the instinct that that the vertices should be a type is very understandable, and the vertices being the whole type has concrete advantages. This is especially the case in algebraic graph theory, where structured functions between graphs are relevant, like when pushing forward a Cayley graph along a group homomorphism.
Luckily, I don't think carrier sets need to conflict with this. One can define typeclass [OnUniv G] which simply contains a proof that the vertex set of G is the universe. There can be instances of this for images of graphs under maps etc, and simp lemmas that supply the mem_vertexSet-like proofs when OnUniv is present. So working with OnUniv is just like working with graphs on a vertex typ

author: Violeta Hernández
content: FYI PR #26966 defines the Dedekind completion and is finally ready

author: Antoine Chambert-Loir
content: I'm very happy about that. It will allow to simplify the end of #31560 where we need to embed a linear order into a complete dense bounded lattice.

author: Violeta Hernández
content: Merged! A good "beginner's project" would be to prove the isomorphism between Dedekind cuts of rationals and the extended reals.

author: Eric Paul
content: I'd be excited to work on proving the isomorphism if no one else is already doing it! Although I wouldn't consider myself a beginner, I haven't really made a PR to mathlib before.

author: Violeta Hernández
content:
Eric Paul ha dicho:

I'd be excited to work on proving the isomorphism if no one else is already doing it! Although I wouldn't consider myself a beginner, I haven't really made a PR to mathlib before.

Feel free to try! My proof sketch goes like this. To define the map DedekindCut ℚ ↪o EReal, you can use docs#DedekindCut.factorEmbedding on the canonical embedding ℚ ↪o EReal. For the map EReal ↪o DedekindCut ℚ, you just send a real number to the sets of rationals less-equal/greater-equal to it (and send ⊥ → ⊥, ⊤ → ⊤). And then proving that these maps are inverse to each other is hopefully not very difficult.

author: Eric Paul
content: I opened a draft PR with a proof in #37071. I wanted to check that this general approach seemed alright before finding everything a good home, name, etc.

author: Bulhwi Cha
content:
/-- If `a` is accessible through a binary relation `r`, then every descending chain starting from
`a` ends at a minimal element of a type `α` with respect to `r`. -/
theorem Acc.descending_chain_ends_at_min_of_acc {α : Sort u} {r : α → α → Prop} {a : α}
    (acc : Acc r a) {f : Nat → α}
    (hsta : f 0 = a)
    (hcon : ∀ ⦃n : Nat⦄, (∀ (y : α), ¬r y (f n)) → f (n + 1) = f n)
    (hdes : ∀ ⦃n : Nat⦄, (¬∀ (y : α), ¬r y (f n)) → r (f (n + 1)) (f n)) :
    ∃ (c : Nat),
      (∀ ⦃y : α⦄, ¬r y (f c)) ∧
      (∀ ⦃n : Nat⦄, n < c → r (f (n + 1)) (f n)) ∧
      ∀ ⦃m : Nat⦄, c ≤ m → f m = f c :=
  sorry


My proof: https://git.sr.ht/~chabulhwi/tpil-solutions/tree/6a827b911134c9ca35a7b6592342cdd31ae19c4e/item/TPIL/Chapter08/Accessibility.lean#L428
Relevant Mathlib API: https://leanprover-community.

author: Bulhwi Cha
content: I've been formalizing the statement of the theorem inaccurately, but the previous versions of the theorem were also true. Nonetheless, those weren't the theorem that I intended to prove.

author: Bulhwi Cha
content:
Consider the following example:
/-- An enumerated type containing four elements. -/
inductive Foo where
  | a
  | b
  | c
  | d

export Foo (a b c d)

/--
Here's a graph that represents this binary relation:
    a
  ↙   ↘
b      c
  ↘
    d
-/
inductive bar : Foo → Foo → Prop where
  | ba : bar b a
  | ca : bar c a
  | db : bar d b

author: Bulhwi Cha
content:
We can prove the theorems listed below:

The elements c and d are minimal elements of Foo.
Every element of Foo is accessible through bar.
The element a has two descending chains that start from it: a, b, d, and a, c.

author: Bulhwi Cha
content: I also wonder whether there are any textbooks that cover the accessibility predicate in detail.

author: Aaron Liu
content: yeah this one seems correct

author: Bulhwi Cha
content:
Bulhwi Cha said:

I also wonder whether there are any textbooks that cover the accessibility predicate in detail.

I haven't seen one that mentions a lemma like Acc.descending_chain_ends_at_min_of_acc.

author: Violeta Hernández
content:
Bulhwi Cha said:

I also wonder whether there are any textbooks that cover the accessibility predicate in detail.

Saying that a is accessible in a preorder is the same thing as saying Iio a is well-founded.

author: Bulhwi Cha
content: That's right! I also wanted to know as many interesting facts as possible, even when the binary relation is not a preorder. Here's my Lean code about the accessibility predicate: https://git.sr.ht/~chabulhwi/tpil-solutions/tree/master/item/TPIL/Chapter08/Accessibility.lean.

author: Timothy Chow
content:
I just tried to copy and paste one of the proofs from the paper (Supplemental Data Figure 6, on the last page of the supplement) and I'm getting an error at "field_simp only". Am I doing something wrong, or has something in Lean changed since the proof was originally generated?
import Mathlib
open Function Polynomial
theorem putnam_2018_b5 (f : (Fin 2 → ℝ) → (Fin 2 → ℝ)) (h₁ : ContDiff ℝ 1 f)
    (h₂ : ∀ x i j, 0 < fderiv ℝ f x (Pi.single i 1) j)
    (h₃ : ∀ x, 0 < fderiv ℝ f x ![1, 0] 0 * fderiv ℝ f x ![0, 1] 1 - (1 / 4) * (fderiv ℝ f x ![1, 0] 1 + fderiv ℝ f x ![0, 1] 0) ^ 2) : Injective f := by
  refine'λa B b=>by_contra (λ (x :) => _)
  let R M:= f$ AffineMap.lineMap B a (M: ℝ)
  suffices(M):deriv R M 0 * ( a 0-B 0)+deriv R M (1) * ( a (1)-B (1)) >(0)
  · suffices:Strict

author: Alex Meiburg
content: There was a change to field_simp, that tactic is substantially different now; generally simp [field] is a closer approximation, so I would try simp only [field] to see if that works.

author: Eric Wieser
content: You can get the Lean version in the paper by looking at the earliest commit of the formal-imo repository

author: Eric Wieser
content: We deliberately did not release plaintext (as opposed to PDF) Putnam proofs, since in the past @George Tsoukalas / @George Tsoukalas has been worried about leakage.

author: Eric Wieser
content: We do also link the precise PutnamBench version (implying a Lean / Mathlib version) near the end of the paper

author: Oliver Tacmon
content:
I’ve recently read the paper “Olympiad-level formal mathematical reasoning with reinforcement learning”.
Looking at AlphaProof’s formal proof of IMO 2024 Problem 2, I noticed that the problem asks us to “find all pairs (a,b)”. The answer is of course the singleton set {(1,1)}, and the Lean code in the paper simply proves that this set is indeed the complete solution set.
However, the formal proof does not capture the “discovery” phase—how one might guess that (1,1) is the only pair in the first place.
My question is: can this “guessing” or discovery process itself be expressed and formalised in Lean?

author: Kim Morrison
content: This has been discussed at length before in this channel. Please see if you can find (and post!) some links. :-)

author: Eric Wieser
content: Note that the IMO code samples in the paper are effectively the same as the ones we released last year; the only change is the removal of the human comments (to make them fit on a page), and obviously the de-verso-ification since putting Verso in a PDF would surely cause a nightmare for the journal!

author: Oliver Tacmon
content: I apologize for not reading the paper carefully enough. I have now noticed the description on page 8: 'Several problems (P1, P2, P5, P6) required identifying the answer (e.g., “Find all functions…”) before constructing a proof. For these, hundreds of candidate answers were generated by querying the public Gemini 1.5 Pro model with Python tool-use enabled and a collection of hand-written examples given as a few-shot prompt [27], successfully guessing the correct answers for all applicable problems.' So I guess I'm no longer that confused.

author: Oliver Tacmon
content: Got it, thank you for the heads-up! I'll search the channel for relevant links :smiling_face_with_hearts:

content:
Filipp said:

Gemini highly praised the Perseus algorithm, which Lean uses for garbage collection.

This is muddled. I think more accurate is to say the Perceus was inspired by Lean. Also it seems a little silly to call either Lean's or Perceus' operation "garbage collection", since the whole point is there is never any garbage.

author: Shreyas Srinivas
content:
Reference counted garbage collection is still garbage collection. 
In this case, the perceus paper is explicit about not providing a cycle collector and "mitigates" cycles rather than solving them.

topic: Why Lean don't use Homotopy Type Theory?
author: James E Hanson
content:
Edward van de Meent said:

I suspect you meant to say doing intuitionistic math is hard in lean 4, right?

What distinction are you drawing here? 'Constructive' and 'intuitionistic' are often synonyms.

topic: Why Lean don't use Homotopy Type Theory?
author: J. J. Issai (project started by GRP)
content: Sometimes the two are not synonyms.  There are those who will happily use LEM in their constructions of things, and consider the result explicit and constructive.  Of course, such as those are unlikely to spend time considering intuitionism.

author: Michael Rothgang
content: If you're building mathlib on your computer, something went wrong in a previous step. You should not need to do so. Can you (1) close VS Code (and check there are no Lean processes running on your computer), (2) delete the .lake directory in your project (for extra caution), (3) open the project again, (4) click on the forall symbol in the top right and fetch the mathlib cache? Wait for that step to complete, then you should not need to build mathlib yourself.

author: Michael Rothgang
content: (Mathlib has hardly any file in the top-level scope, so I don't see how your proposal would help with this.)

author: Paweł Balawender 🇵🇱
content: Okay, that worked - after manually clicking fetch cache, it fetched successfully and after Restart File, no build was running. Is it expected from the user to click Fetch Cache in every Mathlib project or should that be happening by default somehow? Asking to verify if I should proceed to write steps to reproduce bug, or just ignore that

author: Julian Berman
content: Not sure that means anything until we pass all the tests :), possibly I've left something important architecturally out still.

topic: What simple but community-useful task can I take for practic
author: Filipp
content:
Hello!
I want to start learning Lean, but I don't want to spend my time on empty tasks that no one needs. At the same time, I want to contribute to the community. I've heard complaints that Lean doesn't have a developed ecosystem for applied programming. It doesn't even have the most basic libraries!
I'm not a mathematician. I want to write a library for programming, not mathematics.
Tell me, do you know of any low-hanging fruit that no one has picked yet because they haven't gotten around to it?
I have two criteria: 1) Ease of writing. 2) Usefulness and relevance to the industry.
The higher these criteria are, the more excited I am to write this library.

topic: What simple but community-useful task can I take for practic
author: Snir Broshi
content:
These ideas come to mind, though I haven't checked if they exist:

A very performant array DSL (like numpy)
GPU API (like pytorch)
Graphics API (like wgpu)
Dataframe library (like pandas/polars/pyarrow/dask/duckdb)
Arg parser (like click/clap/typer)
TUI library (like tqdm/colorama/rich/textual/blessings)
Plots and graphs library (like matplotlib/graphviz/d3/plotly)
Interactive visualizations/videos library (like manim/remotion/p5js/mafs)

topic: What simple but community-useful task can I take for practic
author: Filipp
content:
What about Serde like framework?
It is a framework for automatic serialization and deserialization.
https://github.com/serde-rs/serde

topic: What simple but community-useful task can I take for practic
author: Arthur Adjedj
content:
Snir Broshi said:


Graphics API (like wgpu)


Bindings for wgpu already exist here. Note that, while the project started 2 years ago as hand-written, the task proved very tedious back then. As such, I experimented a month ago with finishing the bindings using AI, and have since managed to do so. Though I cannot guarantee the absence of potential mistakes and memory leaks, the bindings were enough to produce various example tests that all gave the expected graphical output, including this ray-tracing test.

topic: What simple but community-useful task can I take for practic
author: Snir Broshi
content:
Filipp said:

What about Serde like framework?

I think this overlaps with the FRO too much, since Lean already has JSON and TOML support, and you can already add deriving FromJson to any structure to get what serde-json provides.
I guess you could add support for CSV/YAML/MessagePack. An unofficial protobuf implementation seems to exist.

author: Joachim Breitner
content: Well, we’ll see. It’s not absurd to crush this micro-benchmark (I am experimenting with a pure-debruijn representation, and then it’s trivial here), but crushing this and still being able to handle all the other proofs would be interesting.

author: Jason Rute
content: Is there a clear sense of what the slowest parts of the existing kernels are (for say practical purposes like checking mathlib)?

author: Joachim Breitner
content: There is a stark difference between making a kernel fast for the common case (especially since the common case proof is tailored for the current kernel(s)) and building a kernel that behaves fundamentally, algorithmically different in specific cases (which you will not see in mathlib because they would be slow now so people write different proofs). And even harder to make it good at both?

author: Jason Rute
content: That makes sense.  Does the kernel arena also benchmark for these future optimizations (that may allow people to write proofs differently)?

topic: Blog post: verifying digital circuits with category theory
author: Matt Hunzinger
content:
I'm psyched to share my first blog post, I've been working on denotational semantics for digital circuits using category theory in Lean4
https://matt.hunzinger.me/2026/03/28/circuits.html
In this post I explain how categories relate to circuits and eventually build up to the circuit for an adder for binary numbers. All in all it's been a really fun topic to explore and I hope you enjoy playing around with circuits in Lean too!

author: Joachim Breitner
content: The benchmark mentioned above (app-lam) is exactly one of those that allow a checker with a different binder representation to shine

author: Violeta Hernández
content: then my definition and yours agree :slight_smile:

author: Zikang 'Axi' Yu
content:
Violeta Hernández 說：

Violeta Hernández ha dicho:

stayOn seems somewhat ad-hoc. Presumably what you want to say is Filter.EventuallyConst (iteratedDerivSet s) atTop?

In fact, why not prove ∀ a ≥ (succ #s).ord, sᵈ[a] = sᵈ[b] first, then define the perfect kernel as sᵈ[(succ #s).ord], and finally define the Cantor-Bendixson rank as sInf (iteratedDeriv s ⁻¹' perfectKernel s)?

Then you can only get perfect kernel and set-wise Cantor-Bendixson rank of a closed set, since the first statement holds for closed sets.

author: Violeta Hernández
content: No, that statement should always work. If o ≠ 0 then iteratedDerivSet s o = iteratedDerivSet (closure s) o. So the theorem reduces to the case of a closed set.

author: Violeta Hernández
Violeta Hernández
content:
Violeta Hernández ha dicho:

No, that statement should always work. If o ≠ 0 then iteratedDerivSet s o = iteratedDerivSet (closure s) o. So the theorem reduces to the case of a closed set.

For a T1 space, I should say.

author: Zikang 'Axi' Yu
content: I would prefer to just use "relative" derived set in the definition.

author: Zikang 'Axi' Yu
content: But that will make perfect kernel be "relative" perfect as well, seemingly.

author: Violeta Hernández
content: If the space isn't T0, then you can have this situation: if x and y are topologically indistinguishable, then derivSet {x} = {y} and derivSet {y} = {x}. So the sequence is not eventually constant.

author: Zikang 'Axi' Yu
content:
Violeta Hernández 說：

If the space isn't T0, then you can have this situation: if x and y are topologically indistinguishable, then derivSet {x} = {y} and derivSet {y} = {x}. So the sequence is not eventually constant.

It becomes empty on a limit ordinal, isn't it?

author: Violeta Hernández
content: oh, yeah. or at step 2 under my definition. or at step 1 if we take the relative derived set.

author: Violeta Hernández
content: in fact, if we take relative derived sets, then the sequence of iterated derived sets will always be antitone, then it must be eventually constant because you'd eventually run out of subsets otherwise

author: Violeta Hernández
content: so you don't need the closure condition to define the CB rank. whether it means anything sensible outside of the closed case, that's another thing entirely.

author: Martin Winter
content: I am second-guessing myself  regarding removal of BilinMap. It could be nice to have dot notation available for linear forms of this specific form. Cramming all lemmas in LinearMap with more complicated names seems less useful.

author: Zikang 'Axi' Yu
content: Since these definitions are well-behaved and consistent on closed sets, what we are actually looking for is a reasonable fallback for undefined cases, similar to how Lean defines 1 / 0 = 0. So it seems reasonable to require an IsClosed hypothesis for these statements just as when you use a theorem about division like mul_inv_cancel, it requires a non-zero hypothesis.

author: Violeta Hernández
content: you know, i don't actually think Mathlib has "a monotone/antitone function from Ordinal.{u} into a Small.{u} type is eventually constant"

author: Violeta Hernández
content: it'd be nice to prove that

author: Violeta Hernández
content: I'll PR later (unless you want to try and PR this yourself)

author: Zikang 'Axi' Yu
content: No, I’m happy to leave it to you since I believe you're more familiar with this part.

author: Zikang 'Axi' Yu
James E Hanson
content:
Violeta Hernández said:

It is :) It's also why we call docs#Ordinal.deriv a derivative even when it has nothing to do with derivatives - the name comes from these derived sets.

By a remarkable coincidence the Cantor-Bendixson derivative actually is almost a derivative in an algebraic sense.

author: James E Hanson
content:
Zikang 'Axi' Yu said:

However, as far as I know, it also seems not to have many important applications except for model theory and descriptive set theory.

I don't know if this would be pushing the generality too far, but in model theory we often (implicitly or explicitly) use various generalizations of the Cantor-Bendixson derivative where one removes some other kind of 'small' open set. For instance, you might fix a cardinality κ\kappaκ and remove the union of all open sets of cardinality less than κ\kappaκ at each stage.
How difficult do you think it would be to accommodate this kind of generalization?

topic: Unable to build after lake exe cache get
author: Michael Stoll
content: I think running lake build -H also fixes it without the need to re-download the cache. (Don't ask me what that is doing...)

author: Zikang 'Axi' Yu
content:
James E Hanson 說：

Zikang 'Axi' Yu said:

However, as far as I know, it also seems not to have many important applications except for model theory and descriptive set theory.

I don't know if this would be pushing the generality too far, but in model theory we often (implicitly or explicitly) use various generalizations of the Cantor-Bendixson derivative where one removes some other kind of 'small' open set. For instance, you might fix a cardinality κ\kappaκ and remove the union of all open sets of cardinality less than κ\kappaκ at each stage.
How difficult do you think it would be to accommodate this kind of generalization?

Actually, that's exactly my motivation for formalizing Cantor-Bendixson analysis. This is essentially related to the connection between Cantor-Bendixson rank and Morl
